title: Bajan fish cakes
subtitle: Barbadian salt cod and seasoning fritters
excerpt: Salt cod flaked into a seasoned flour batter with spring onion, thyme, scotch bonnet, and black pepper, then fried into small, golden, crispy-edged rounds. Bajan fish cakes are the most famous Barbadian street food — sold from vendors and eaten at the beach, at fetes, and at the monthly Oistins Fish Festival. They are eaten hot, plain or with salt bread, and are always part of the Sunday morning spread.
sourceNotes: Bajan fish cakes are made from salted and preserved salt cod (saltfish), which was the primary protein available to enslaved people on Barbados and throughout the Caribbean. The preserved fish tradition has been part of Barbadian cooking since the 17th century. The fish cake recipe is documented in Barbadian cookbooks from the early 20th century and is considered one of the defining dishes of Bajan cuisine.

recipe.batchNotes: Fry in batches. Keep warm in a low oven.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The saltfish must be soaked 24 to 48 hours before use. Change the water 3 to 4 times.

content.text: The batter is thicker than a fritter batter and more like a dropped scone batter — firm enough to scoop into rounds that hold their shape. A batter that is too thin produces flat, lacy fish cakes that stick to the pan; too thick and they are doughy and don't cook through.

The Bajan difference from Jamaican saltfish fritters is the black pepper and shadow beni (culantro). In the absence of shadow beni, use extra spring onion and a small amount of fresh coriander. The fish must be properly desalinated — taste a piece of the boiled cod before making the batter. It should be pleasantly savoury, not aggressively salty.

Soak and boil the {{salt-cod}} as described in the makeAheadNotes, then flake it finely, removing skin and bones. Combine {{plain-flour}} and {{baking-powder}} in a bowl. Beat the {{eggs}} and {{whole-milk}} together and add to the flour. Mix to a smooth thick batter. Fold in the flaked saltfish, {{spring-onion}}, {{scotch-bonnet}}, {{thyme}}, {{coriander}} if using, and {{black-pepper}}. The batter should be thick enough to scoop. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a pan to about 3 cm depth over medium-high heat. Drop heaped tablespoons of batter into the oil and flatten slightly. Fry for 2 to 3 minutes per side until deeply golden. Drain and serve hot.

items.fix: Soak for the full 48 hours with 4 water changes and always taste the cooked fish before making the batter

items.symptom: Fish cakes falling apart in the oil
items.cause: Batter too thin or not enough egg to bind
items.fix: The batter must be thick enough to hold together when scooped. Add a tablespoon of flour if it seems too wet

items.cause: Oil not hot enough or too shallow
items.fix: The oil should be at 170°C. Shallow frying at this depth and temperature gives a crispy exterior in the correct time

content.text: Bajan fish cakes are the street food of Barbados. At the Oistins Fish Festival they are made in vast quantities; at the beach bars they are eaten with salt bread and Banks beer; at roadside vendors they are wrapped in paper and eaten by hand. The recipe is one of the most widely reproduced Barbadian recipes outside the island and appears in Caribbean cookbooks throughout the diaspora. In Britain it is a staple of Barbadian home cooking and appears at Caribbean food events, recognisably distinct from the Jamaican stamp-and-go version by the black pepper and coriander seasoning.
